title: On a Problem of Polya and Some of its Evolutions
speaker: Umberto Zannier
abstract:
Around 1921 Polya asked about the algebraicity of the indefinite integral of an algebraic function, in terms of the integrality of the coefficients in a power-series expansion. Polya nicely answered the special case of rational functions, using Fermat's congruence. Though the formulation was very simple, the general case was solved only much later by transcendence techniques.  The question is also linked with a simply stated well-known local-global conjecture of Grothendieck on linear differential equations over polynomial rings, and with other number-theoretical issues.
We shall survey on the topic, also illustrating a novel question arising from a different possible approach to Polya's issue, and some answers given more recently by Katz. If time allows we shall sketch a recent argument to prove a strengthening of the main statements.

proof_stages.label: Pólya problem as exactness of algebraic differentials
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up algebraic power series over Q, Eisenstein's finite-denominator/global-boundedness theorem, and reformulates Pólya's question as asking whether a formally exact differential with integral primitive is actually exact on the algebraic curve.

proof_stages.label: Pólya's rational-function proof by congruences
proof_stages.summary: For rational functions, partial fractions reduce the obstruction to logarithmic terms, and congruences for the exponential-polynomial coefficients force a Vandermonde-type determinant contradiction unless that obstruction vanishes.

proof_stages.label: Geometric mod-p exactness and its limits
proof_stages.summary: The rational proof is reinterpreted as using only exactness of reductions modulo primes, via Cartier/Honda-Katz results identifying local power-series exactness with exactness on the reduced curve, which extends genus zero but fails in genus one unless stronger prime-power information is used.

proof_stages.label: Chudnovsky-André solution and density methods
proof_stages.summary: Known proofs of Pólya's problem are presented as consequences of Chudnovsky and André's G-function/transcendence methods, relying on auxiliary functions, p-adic radii of convergence, Jacobian uniformization, and density greater than one half of good primes.

proof_stages.label: Link with the Grothendieck-Katz p-curvature conjecture
proof_stages.summary: Because algebraic functions satisfy linear differential equations, Pólya's problem is placed beside the Grothendieck-Katz local-global principle asserting that sufficiently many mod-p solution systems should imply algebraic solutions in characteristic zero.

proof_stages.label: Infinitely many primes with all prime powers
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states ongoing work suggesting that for Pólya's problem one can replace positive-density primes by infinitely many primes if one assumes the corresponding prime-power integrality/exactness, while explaining that analogous statements fail for more general differential equations.

proof_stages.label: Cartier-operator and genus-one proof strategy
proof_stages.summary: An alternative geometric approach uses the Cartier operator, zero residues, and the finite-dimensional quotient of differentials modulo exact forms, reducing in genus one to relations between differentials of the first and second kind and then using supersingular/formal-group arguments to rule out the obstruction.

examples.name: Logarithmic integral of a rational function
examples.note: A rational/algebraic derivative whose formal integral is a logarithm illustrates the failure of the naive converse and motivates adding Pólya's integrality condition.
examples.engagement_reason: he integrates a rational/algebraic example to obtain a logarithm and later works through the rational-function case via partial fractions, logarithmic terms, coefficient sums, and congruences

passes.reason: Its logarithmic integral has a rational/algebraic derivative but is not algebraic, refuting the converse claim.
A logarithmic primitive of a rational function is used to show that an algebraic derivative need not have an algebraic primitive.
A logarithmic integral of a rational function is used to refute the converse that an algebraic derivative forces an algebraic primitive.
A logarithmic primitive of a rational/algebraic function is an ordinary example used to refute the converse that an algebraic derivative has an algebraic primitive.
An ordinary logarithmic primitive of a rational function is used to show that an algebraic derivative need not have an algebraic primitive.
A logarithmic primitive of a rational function is used to refute the converse that an algebraic derivative must have an algebraic primitive.
A rational/algebraic derivative with logarithmic primitive is used to refute the converse that an algebraic derivative has an algebraic primitive.
used as the basic case where an algebraic/rational derivative has a logarithmic nonalgebraic primitive, disproving the converse.
used to refute the converse that an algebraic derivative has an algebraic integral, while a logarithmic integral of a rational function is structurally ordinary
A rational integrand whose primitive is logarithmic is used to refute the converse that an algebraic derivative must have an algebraic primitive.
Used to refute the converse that an algebraic derivative must have an algebraic primitive; structurally it is an ordinary logarithmic primitive of a rational function.
used to show that an algebraic rational derivative may integrate to a logarithm that is not algebraic, with no special intrinsic structure beyond a typical rational-logarithmic integral

examples.name: Supersingular elliptic-curve differential
examples.note: An elliptic curve with a differential of the first kind that becomes exact modulo the supersingular primes, for example primes congruent to 3 mod 4 in a CM case, shows that infinitely many mod-p exact reductions alone cannot force exactness in characteristic zero.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies the genus-one Cartier-operator analysis to elliptic differentials, relating exactness at supersingular primes to the Cartier constants such as the trace term vanishing

passes.reason: The differential is exact modulo infinitely many supersingular primes though not exact in characteristic zero, and supersingularity is an extremal p-rank/formal-height condition.
The supersingular elliptic differential lies at the p-rank-zero supersingular extreme and is used to show that exactness modulo infinitely many primes need not imply characteristic-zero exactness.

The supersingular elliptic differential is an extremal characteristic-p case, with minimal p-rank/maximal formal height, used to show that exactness for infinitely many primes alone need not imply exactness in characteristic zero.
Supersingular reduction is an extremal elliptic-curve phenomenon, and the differential is used to show exactness modulo infinitely many primes need not imply exactness in characteristic zero.
Supersingularity is the p-rank-zero/Newton-polygon extreme for elliptic curves, and the differential is used to refute that exactness for infinitely many primes suffices.
The supersingular elliptic reduction is a p-rank-zero extreme and is used to show that exactness modulo infinitely many primes need not imply characteristic-zero exactness.
Used to show infinitely many primes alone do not force characteristic-zero exactness, with supersingularity giving an extremal p-rank/Newton-polygon case.
used to show exactness modulo infinitely many supersingular primes need not imply characteristic-zero exactness, with supersingularity an extremal p-rank/Newton-polygon condition.
used to show that infinitely many primes alone do not suffice, and supersingularity is an extremal elliptic-curve reduction condition

The elliptic-curve differential is exact modulo infinitely many supersingular primes but not in characteristic zero, refuting that infinitely many primes suffice; supersingularity is the maximal-height/p-rank-zero extreme for elliptic curves.
Used to show that exactness modulo infinitely many primes need not imply characteristic-zero exactness; supersingularity is the maximal-height/minimal-p-rank boundary case for elliptic curves.
used to show that exactness modulo infinitely many supersingular primes need not imply exactness in characteristic zero, and supersingularity is the p-rank-zero/Newton-slope extremal elliptic case

examples.name: The series sum (2^n-2)x^n/n
examples.note: This Fermat-congruence example has the desired divisibility at prime indices while its derivative is algebraic, showing that testing only single prime multiples is too weak.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses the specific series as a counterexample by differentiating it to get an algebraic function and checking the prime-coefficient integrality issue

passes.reason: Fermat gives integrality at prime indices while the integral is still a nonalgebraic logarithm, so checking only primes fails.
The series has prime-index integrality by Fermat while its logarithmic primitive is not algebraic, so it refutes checking only single prime multiples.
The series with coefficients (2^n-2)/n is an ordinary logarithmic power series used to show that checking only prime-index divisibility is insufficient.
The log-type series is structurally ordinary but is used to show that checking only prime indices/single multiples of primes cannot suffice.
This ordinary logarithmic power series has the prime-index integrality forced by Fermat but is used to show that checking only single prime multiples cannot suffice.
The series has algebraic/rational derivative and prime-index integrality by Fermat, showing that checking only prime indices cannot suffice.
It shows checking only prime indices is insufficient, since Fermat divisibility holds at primes while the logarithmic series is not an algebraic primitive.
Fermat divisibility makes the prime-index tests pass while the logarithmic primitive is still nonalgebraic, so primes alone do not suffice.
used to show that checking only prime indices cannot suffice, while the logarithmic series itself is not structurally special
The series passes all prime-index divisibility tests by Fermat while its logarithmic primitive is not algebraic, showing single primes alone cannot suffice.

Used to show that checking only prime indices cannot suffice, since its derivative is rational/algebraic while prime-index divisibility holds; the series is otherwise a standard logarithmic rational series.
used to show that checking only prime-index congruences is insufficient, while the logarithmic rational series itself is structurally ordinary

examples.name: Binomial/hypergeometric function with irrational algebraic exponent
examples.note: A function such as a binomial series with exponent sqrt(2) has p-integral coefficients for infinitely many split primes but is not algebraic, showing that the speaker's prime-power principle does not extend naively to general differential equations.
examples.engagement_reason: he cites the irrational-algebraic-exponent binomial/hypergeometric example as having p-integral coefficients for split primes and being nonalgebraic without deriving it

passes.reason: A binomial series with irrational algebraic exponent is nonalgebraic yet p-integral at infinitely many split primes, defeating the naive prime-integrality criterion.
The binomial hypergeometric series with algebraic irrational exponent is p-integral for infinitely many split primes but is not algebraic.
The binomial/hypergeometric function with irrational algebraic exponent is a standard family member used to show that p-adic integrality for infinitely many split primes does not force algebraicity.
A binomial/hypergeometric function with algebraic irrational exponent is a standard nonalgebraic member used against a naive prime-integrality criterion for broader differential equations.
A standard binomial/hypergeometric series with irrational algebraic exponent is nonalgebraic despite p-integrality at split primes, refuting the proposed prime-set principle.
A binomial hypergeometric function with irrational algebraic exponent has p-integral coefficients for infinitely many split primes but is not algebraic.
The binomial hypergeometric series with algebraic irrational exponent is p-integral at split primes but nonalgebraic, obstructing the proposed weakening for general differential equations.
the binomial series with irrational algebraic exponent is p-integral at split primes but not algebraic, refuting the analogous integrality criterion for general differential equations.
used as a non-algebraic function with p-adically integral coefficients at infinitely many split primes, but it belongs to a standard binomial/hypergeometric family

The binomial series with algebraic irrational exponent has p-integral coefficients at infinitely many split primes but is not algebraic, obstructing an infinite-prime criterion for general differential equations.

Used to refute an infinite-prime integrality criterion for general differential equations, since the split-prime p-integral binomial series with algebraic irrational exponent is not algebraic; it is not structurally sporadic or extremal.
used as a nonalgebraic binomial solution with p-integral coefficients at infinitely many split primes, an ordinary rank-one binomial/hypergeometric example

examples.name: Apéry sequences for zeta(3)
examples.note: Apéry's integer sequences are cited as a striking case where one solution of a differential equation has integral coefficients while another solution of the same equation loses integrality.
examples.engagement_reason: he only refers to Apéry’s zeta(3) sequences as striking related integer-coefficient examples and says there is no time to write them down

passes.reason: The Apéry integer solution is cited as an obstruction to a naive differential-equation extension, since integrality breaks down for the companion solution.
The Apéry sequence example is cited as a striking obstruction to extending integrality from one differential-equation solution to the full system.
The Apéry sequence example is used to illustrate failure of integrality for the companion solution of the same differential equation, not as a sporadic structural exception.
The Apéry integer solutions are special but not classification-exceptional, and here illustrate the failure of extending integrality of one solution to all solutions of the differential equation.
Apéry-number generating functions are cited as nonalgebraic integral solutions showing that such integrality phenomena do not by themselves force algebraicity of the differential equation.
The Apéry integer solution is invoked as another case where integrality of a special solution fails to extend to the full differential-equation setting.
Apéry integer solutions are cited as an obstruction to naive integrality criteria, since another solution of the same differential equation loses integrality.
cited as a striking related integral G-function solution, with a nonintegral companion solution, rather than as a structural exception or direct counterexample.
used to illustrate failure of a naive integrality principle for general differential equations, while Apéry sequences are special but not sporadic or extremal in the strict structural sense
The Apéry integer solution illustrates that one solution of a differential equation can have integral coefficients while companion solutions lose integrality, blocking a naive integrality criterion.

Cited as a case where one integral solution of a differential equation does not make the companion solution integral, blocking a naive Grothendieck-type extension; Apéry sequences are special but part of systematic Apéry-like recurrences.
